steps.title: Find the mode — highest frequency
steps.content:
The mode is the most frequently occurring value. Looking at the frequency column: age 18 appears 6 times, which is more than any other age. All other ages appear 5 times or fewer.

$$\text{mode} = 18$$

steps.title: Find the median — locate the middle values
steps.content:
With 20 students (even count), the median is the average of the 10th and 11th values in order. Counting cumulatively from the table:
  • Positions 1–6: age 18
  • Positions 7–11: age 19
  • Positions 12–15: age 20
  • Positions 16–20: ages 21, 22, 23, 30
The 10th student is age 19, and the 11th student is also age 19.

$$\text{median} = \frac{19 + 19}{2} = 19$$

steps.title: Find the mean — sum and divide by 20
Compare and order the three measures
steps.content:
We now have:
  • Mode = 18
  • Median = 19
  • Mean = 20

The distribution is skewed right (one student at age 30 pulls the mean up), which causes the mean to exceed the median. The mode is the lowest because the youngest age has the highest frequency. That matches choice A — A is correct.
